title: Classic mince pies
subtitle: Short, buttery pastry cases filled with spiced mincemeat
excerpt: Small individual pastry pies filled with shop-bought or homemade mincemeat and dusted with icing sugar. The pastry is a sweet shortcrust: buttery, tender, and just firm enough to hold a mincemeat filling without going soggy.
sourceNotes: Mince pies have been a British Christmas staple since the medieval period. Early versions contained actual meat alongside dried fruit; by the nineteenth century the meat content had largely given way to suet-based mincemeat. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives a mincemeat recipe with suet, dried fruit, and spirits close to the modern form. The small, individual pastry case filled with mincemeat became standard in Victorian domestic baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen in a 180°C oven for 10 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The pastry makes about 18 cases; bake in two batches if needed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake up to 2 days ahead; store in a tin and dust with icing sugar just before serving. The pastry stays crisp.

content.text: The pastry needs to be short enough to snap when bitten but firm enough to hold a spoonful of mincemeat without collapsing when lifted. A ratio of 350 g flour to 175 g butter, with icing sugar for tenderness, achieves that. Fill the cases to just below the top of the pastry rim; overfilled pies leak mincemeat under the lid and can stick to the tin. Brush the lid with beaten egg for colour, not just milk.

Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the egg and cold water and mix until the dough just comes together. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 190°C fan and grease a 12-hole muffin tin.

Roll the chilled pastry to about 3 mm on a lightly floured surface. Cut rounds using a 9 cm cutter for the bases and 7 cm rounds (or stars) for the lids. Press the larger rounds into the holes of the muffin tin. Put a teaspoon of mincemeat into each case, filling to just below the pastry rim. Dampen the edges of the pastry with water and press the lid rounds on top, sealing the edges. Brush the tops with beaten egg. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes, until the pastry is golden. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Dust generously with icing sugar before serving.
